text: The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to dissect severe adhesions between the abdominal wall and the omentum, carefully separating these structures to access the target lesion.

The surgeon dissects the mesenteric attachment, leaving the paraortic lymph node tissue intact around the inferior vena cava, while performing cohormabilization without peeling off the layer behind the duodenum.
The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava, revealing the left renal vein as it flows into the inferior vena cava behind the duodenum and the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects around the left renal vein, exposing the superior mesenteric artery and associated nerve tissue. A metastatic lymph node, approximately two centimeters in size, is revealed, surrounded by the pancreatic parenchyma, polar vein, right crux of the diaphragm, and liver caudate lobe.
The surgeon clips and cuts the feeding artery of the metastatic lymph node to facilitate its removal with minimal bleeding.
